sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
After a few days, being in Lodz with my girlfriend, I persuaded my friend-- my mother's friend who was from [NON-ENGLISH], and he was carrying a shotgun.
And he had authority for the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH].
I persuaded him that I want to go home to my apartment from where I came from, where I lived before the war.
But my friend was very skeptical that I should go alone, because when I came to Lodz, the Poles were as cruel to Jews the same way as they were before the war.
For instance, when I was liberated, I had no birth certificate.
So I figured when I am home I will go to the magistrate, to have my birth certificate that I should have for the future.
To have my birth certificate, I had to go back to my house, to go to the janitor that they had the ledger of all the occupants from the building.
I came to my court where I used to live.

And instead to go into the basement where the janitor used to live, now he lived in one of the most prominent Jewish homes in an apartment that the most prominent, most wealthy Jew from the whole court used to live.
I came into his apartment.
And I told him who I am, and he said-- looked at me, and he says, [NON-ENGLISH].
You are still alive?
So I disregarded it.
And he asked me what I want.
And I told him what I am looking for.
And sure enough, he pulled out a ledger.
And I found in the ledger my whole family, the names.
And I got what I needed.
And when I came to the magistrate, to the city hall, to have my birth certificate, they told me that during the war all the documents were burned that they don't have any records of anything.
Over the next few days, I persuaded my friend to go with me to visit my home, because when I was liberated, I didn't even have pictures of my mother or father or my brothers.
I had no pictures at all.
And I longed to see their faces even on the picture.
Because at that time, I lost hope that I would ever see them again.
Because the daily visits that I paid to the list, and I checked it every day, I couldn't find no names of ours.
That friend of ours picked me up one day, and we went to our home.
And I told my friend, why I am going home.
I'm going because I want a picture.
That's number one.
And then I told him about what my father did that my father buried a [NON-ENGLISH], and he buried a lot of jewelry and silverware and stuff, whatever we had in our house.
And we went to our house.
I came up to my apartment.
As I opened the door, I was flabbergasted.
When I entered my kitchen-- my mother was a religious woman.
In 1935, my uncle from France came on the [NON-ENGLISH], on a visit to Lodz, and brought my mother a lot of precious mementos.
One of the things that I remember was a beautiful tablecloth, for Yom Tov and for Shabbat, that my mother used to use for special occasions like that.
When I entered my apartment and I walked in the kitchen, a little boy sat on my kitchen table.
And that precious tablecloth that I remembered it so well was spread on the table, and that little boy was sitting all filthy on the middle of the table on that tablecloth.
I tried to keep strong.
And I proceeded in the kitchen.
We had a very big wall credenza, white credenza with glass enclosed.
And I remember my mother always used to have little curtains made in the glass windows in that wall unit.
But on the bottom of wall unit, on the credenza, there were two big drawers that my mother used to keep all the family albums and certain pictures, and certain valuable papers.
Disregarding the woman, and disregarding whatever happened with the table, I went straight to the credenza.
And the woman approaches me, and she says, what are you looking for?
And my friend, she was a little bit skeptical to ask me.
Because she was afraid that my friend was wearing a band, and he was the authority.
But she still asked me what I am looking for.
So my friend told her that this is my apartment that I lived before the war, and all the things in it are mine.
So she became scared.
And she says, oh, you could take whatever you want.
I took care of it.
You could take whatever you want.
So I said to her, I don't need anything.
I just-- I am looking in the drawers for pictures of my parents.
So she said to me, oh forget it, she said.
We didn't know that you're going to come back alive.
We burned them.
When we took over the apartment, we burned everything what was in it.
So I became very upset, and I started to cry.
So I grabbed the arm of my friend.
And then I said to him, let's go into the coal bin, where my father buried the [NON-ENGLISH].
When I came into the coal bin, not just that the floor was ripped up, but even the walls were missing.
The only thing that I found in the coal bin, because as I said I had a little brother who was a year old, I found a white enamel little bathtub that my mother used to bathe the baby.
I found this little bathtub.
And in it, I found the clothes from the baby.
That's the only things that I found.
I went back into my apartment.
The only thing that I took with me was on the wall, staring into my face, my mandolin that I played since childhood, and my brother's violin.
I went to the wall, and I took off my banjo.
And there were still the braids that I used to make, the colorful braids that I used to braid them for the mandolin with all the colors.
And the woman said to me, oh that's yours?
I says, yes, that's mine.
I said the violin is mine too, but you could have it.
I took the mandolin, and I grabbed my friend's arm, and I said, let's get out of here.
Because I couldn't take the pain anymore.
And that's the last time I saw my home.
And that's, with this mandolin, I was on my way to Austria, and I was sitting on the train when somebody sat on it and broke it.
And I was devastated.
Because it was the only thing left from my home and from my youth.
When I arrived in Salzburg it was a displaced person camp.
We had food.

The authority over there, the UNRRA and the IRO International Refugee Organization, took our names, took whatever we remembered from before the war, family, whatever way we had in Europe, or family that were in the United States, or whatever, on the continent.
Whatever I remembered, I gave all the names.
It happened that I remembered from before the war that my mother's brother lived in Paris.
That my grandfather, my mother's father, lived in Brazil; I did not know addresses.
But I remembered that they lived in Brazil.
And I gave all that.
I stayed in Salzburg till a group of [?
Chayalim ?] came from Israel.
And they told us that we had a place in this world that there is Palestine for us.
Because we used to be called staatenlos, people without a country.
Because we were so against Poland.
We were so bitter of the treatments that they did-- what they did to us, and the collaboration that they did with the Germans that we hated them on the same level as the Germans, even more.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The [?
Chayalim ?] organized a group, and we went from Salzburg.
They took us to Udine.
This is the border of Italy and Austria.
We went to Udine, and then from Udine, we went between the mountains by mountain cable cars, with the mountains.
And then we arrived to Udine.
From Udine we went to Bologna.
That was also the UNRRA.
That was also the IRO, the International Refugee Organization.
They fed us.
They gave us different clothes, the UNRRA.
They gave us soap to wash.
We were very good treated.
When we arrived to Bologna, the UNRRA and the [?
Chayalim ?] organized a whole group to go where there Israeli boats supposed to take us with the [NON-ENGLISH] to Israel.
We wound up from Bologna.
We went to Santa Maria di Bagni.
This was a camp, a displaced person camp.
And we were also given-- in that place we did not live in barracks.
We lived in Italian homes, the villas, empties villas.
But it was taken care from the UNRRA and the IRO.
But we were taken here by the Jewish Federation and the UNRRA.
We were being given food daily.
And we were waiting.
We were told that when the quota of people were organized, we will be transferred to Palestine.
In the meantime, while we're waiting there were also formed cultural groups.
They were forming a library, which we didn't have books for so many years.
We formed a library.
They have formed a dramatic group that a friend of mine suggested one day that I should go to a rehearsal.
That day I went to a rehearsal to the dramatic group, and I met my future husband.
He was the director of the Jewish theater.
He formed a group, a dramatic group.
He was born in Bialystok.
And his father was a director from the Jewish theater in Bialystok.
And from the memory, he knew all plays from the memory.
And he dictated another friend to write down all the plays.
And he took children like me, like 10 others, and taught them how to play and directed them.
And he wrote a lot of plays from the war.
He wrote a lot of plays.
One of them, the first one was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he directed it, and we became acquainted.
And automatically we fell in love.
And I joined the group, and I played with my husband on the stage.
We went from one displaced person camp to the other, entertaining displaced person people like us, we entertained them.
And my husband from the beginning, he even entertained the American troops.
Because he was liberated by the Americans.
He was liberated from Auschwitz to Mauthausen.
And he was liberated.
Because he went to a krankenhaus.
He had typhoid right after he was liberated.
Yeah.
He was liberated from a krankenhaus in Germany.
Krankenhaus is a hospital in Germany.
And then he arrived to Italy.
And I met him in the dramatic group.
And we kept together with the dramatic group.
And Sol and I would formed a kibbutz aliyah.
And from the beginning, they gave us rations of food.
And I, being that I was brought up in a kosher home, I longed to have a kosher kibbutz.
And I went to Rome, with Sol.
We went to the UNRRA.
And we worked on it they should give us dry products, and we will be in charge.
And I will cook.
We were 120 youngsters, boys and girls, and formed a beautiful kibbutz aliyah.
And we made a kosher kibbutz.
Came Friday, I had to pluck the chickens, came a shohet from Rome.
And I used to pluck the chicken and make kosher, and used to cook for Shabbat, and I churned and bake and cook.
And we had a kosher kibbutz.
And Friday night my husband was in charge for all the Shabbats.
I wasn't even married then.
But I lit candles every Friday night in memory of my parents.
Because a lot of kids asked me, you have no right to light candles, because you are not married.
And I said, those candles are the memory of my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We formed a kibbutz.
It was beautiful.
And at one point the kibbutz selected me as the outstanding member of the kibbutz, gave me a [NON-ENGLISH] that I should be the first one to land in the state of Israel.
Israel was not Israel at that time.
But I was supposed to go with a group, [?
Chayalim, ?] dressed as a chayal, and to go to Israel.
They gave me a [NON-ENGLISH].
They made me a beautiful good-bye evening in kibbutz.
And I was selected to go.
The [?
Chayalim ?] picked me up at night to go to the boat.
And a group of other kids-- I came from that kibbutz.
But there were other kids from different kibbutzim.
We came to the boat.
Unfortunately, I guess it was meant to be in life that came another group, pregnant women.
And Israel wanted the kids to be born in Israel.
And we came back to the kibbutz aliyah.
And the pregnant women left for Israel.
They wound up in Cyprus.
But they were going to Israel.
And I remained in kibbutz with my future husband.
From there, we were transferred.
The camp was transferred to Bari.
We didn't know at that time where we want to go.
But my family from Brazil and my family from France found me through the UNRRA newspapers.
And they send me telegrams with money, and they send me from the Red Cross packages of food to the camp.
I wrote them letters that I am not here alone that I have a boyfriend.
And my family started to write letters that the Yiddish maidel doesn't have a boyfriend.
You will remain in life alone, and if you have a boyfriend you have to get married.
So my uncle and my grandfather in Brazil stayed in touch with the consulate in Rome, the Brazilian consul.
Because they send me papers to go to Brazil.
And they insisted that I should come to Brazil that I have a big family.
And they wanted to have this [NON-ENGLISH] to take me to the chuppah.
On the other hand, my uncle in Paris insisted that I should come to Paris that my mother wanted him to have the mitzvah to take me to the chuppah.
Between the times my husband found out that he has family here in the United States.
And as it came, my husband became obsessed that he wanted to go to the United States.
By the time were made up our mind to go to Brazil, Jews were not allowed to go to Brazil.
And we couldn't go to Brazil.
Anyway, we made up our mind that instead to go to Brazil to wait till Jews were allowed, but I wrote to my uncle that for money you could make false papers as an Aryan to go to Brazil.
And my grandfather became frantic.
He wrote to me in a letter that nobody in my family is [NON-ENGLISH], converted.
That I shouldn't dare do that.
That he wouldn't want to have on his conscious to do that.
Well, I didn't.
Instead we went to Paris.
My uncle from Paris sent me a tourist visa to Paris.
And Sol and I, we went to Paris.
And we had a complete wedding in Paris.
By that time my uncle who was liberated in Russia came to Paris too.
Because he was looking for me in Poland.
And he couldn't find me.
So he came to his brother and both brothers took me to the chuppah.
And I got married in Paris And after Paris, we were there three months, and we came back to Italy.
And then they called us to the visa to come to the States.
And it took quite a while.
What year was this?
I beg your pardon?
That was-- we were married in 1947, August 21, 1947.
It would be my 50th wedding anniversary this year.

But anyway, coming from Paris to Italy when they called us to the centers for paperwork to go to the United States, my husband's uncle that lived in Passaic vouched for us that he will see to it that we had a job when we come.
But the organization IRO, the International Organization for Refugees, brought us to the states.
And on November 21, 1949, we came to the United States.
But before that, we went to the embarkation center.
We lived in Bari.
We lived in Bari first in the camp.
Then from Bari, they transferred our camp to Florence.
But while I lived in Bari, something was missing in my life that I never finished my education.
Wherever there was a course or school opened, I went to take lessons.
I learned English, basic English, whenever I was able to.
I learned Italian.
I went to the Italian school.
I went to ORT.
I took a course in fashion designing.
I took a course in nursing.
Then the camp was transferred to Florence.
And I continue my schooling and whatever education I did in Florence, and I also continued with ORT.
I finished fashion designing.
And I also went for nursing.
From Florence, they transferred, the International Refuge Organization, transferred us to Ladispoli.
This was also a good DP place for displaced persons people.
We lived in Ladispoli a short while.
And from Ladispoli they transferred us when they called us for our papers to go to Bagnoli.
Bagnoli is the embarkation center that transferred people to the boat, to Naples.
We were in Bagnoli in an embarkation center waiting for the boat.
We went through very rigorous paperwork, investigations.
Health-wise they checked us by so many doctors, which later we found out that only Jews were interrogated that way.
Because on the boat that we were on, Marine Jumper, there were a lot of Germans, a lot of non-Jews.
They never had that problem.
Only Jewish people had that terrible problem to be interrogated that way.
But we came.
After 11 days we arrived to the United States.
Very good.
Very, very good.
When I learned the language, and I told them that I am ebreo, it means Jewish, they couldn't understand the difference.
And we met a lot of Jews, Italian Jews, that they lived through the war.
They never encountered hardships like we did.
